text: Graham Wallas, in his 1926 book "The Art of Thought," proposed a four-stage model of creativity: preparation, incubation, illumination, and verification. The incubation stage — the period of apparent idleness between effortful work and the sudden solution — was, Wallas believed, when the most important cognitive activity occurred, invisible to introspection. What neuroscience has since confirmed is not merely that incubation helps, but why: the prefrontal cortex, which enforces familiar associative pathways, relaxes its gatekeeping during rest, allowing the brain to surface remote, weakly-connected associations it would otherwise suppress as irrelevant.
question: According to the passage, why does incubation help creativity, according to neuroscience?

options.text: The hippocampus replays relevant memories at higher speed during rest.
The brain enters a theta-wave state that enhances long-range connectivity.
The prefrontal cortex relaxes its gatekeeping, allowing remote associations to surface.
The default mode network generates novel metaphors by combining semantic fields.
